one_line_takeaway: DISC is a distortion-free LLM watermark that embeds several bits of forensic meta-information at O(L^2) decoding cost, reaching 0 BER in simulation within 6-20 tokens for 1-4 bit messages -- but it is validated only on synthetic binary sequences, not real LLM text, and against no empirical baseline.
key_finding: In simulation, DISC decodes the embedded message with vanishing bit error rate after a modest number of tokens: a 1-bit watermark reaches 0 BER over a text of only 6 real tokens, and a 4-bit watermark reaches 0 BER at 20 real tokens (BER measured over 10,000 runs per text length, m from 1 to 4, one real token = 17 bits). BER drops sharply as text length grows for all four message sizes. Separately, the authors show decoding complexity can be reduced to O(L^2) and made independent of 2^m by exploiting the known shape of the score-vs-shift curve rather than exhaustively testing every candidate message. The scheme is proven distortion-free (output distribution identical to the base LLM) while carrying multiple bits, which the authors position as the first such combination.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is DISC (Distribution Interval Shift Coding), which the authors claim is the first efficient multi-bit distortion-free watermarking scheme for LLMs. It extends the zero-bit distortion-free scheme of Christ et al. (2023) in three concrete ways that are the paper's own work: (1) it embeds m bits of meta-information (e.g., model name/version, generation time) by shifting the sampling interval of a binarized token by an amount delta_M = M*delta with delta = 2^-m, while proving the shift keeps the output distribution identical to the base LLM (distortion-free); (2) it introduces a message-parameterized score function and p-value test so the decoder jointly estimates the initialization point n* and the embedded message M*; and (3) it gives an efficient decoder that avoids exhaustive search over all 2^m messages, reducing decoding to O(L^2) independent of the number of embedded bits. The authors also present two corrections/modifications to the prior Christ et al. scheme as their own contribution: correcting a flaw in that scheme's statistical test/decoder, and feeding the PRF a context n-gram S_{i,h} = W[i-h:i-1] rather than the token index i, which they argue defends against a trivial deletion attack (deleting one token would otherwise desynchronize detection). The formal 'watermarking mapping rule' abstraction (Definitions 2.4, 4.1; Propositions 2.5, 4.2) that unifies distortion-free schemes is also introduced here.
threat_model: The security setting is provenance and attribution of AI-generated text, not confidentiality or model integrity. Defender: an LLM operator who wants any generated text to be later verifiable as machine-generated AND to carry embedded meta-information (model/version/time) for forensic traceback of misuse (misinformation, academic cheating, influence operations). Detection requires a secret key shared between encoder and decoder (the PRF key sk), so it is a keyed watermark, not a public detector. Adversary goals implicitly considered: (a) an adversary who would exploit quality distortion of naive watermarks to statistically detect/strip the mark -- addressed by the distortion-free guarantee that watermarked output is distributionally identical to the base model; and (b) an adversary performing edits, specifically single-token deletion, which motivates the context-n-gram PRF input. Note the paper does NOT empirically evaluate an active removal/paraphrase adversary; robustness to deletion is argued by design, and false-positive control on human text is handled statistically via the FPR-bounded threshold (properties II/III).
limitations: 1) Experiments are pure simulation on synthetic binary sequences with randomly generated per-bit probabilities and random u values -- there is NO evaluation on an actual LLM's outputs, no text-quality/perplexity check, and no human-vs-AI false-positive measurement on real corpora, so real-world detection and true distortion-freeness are only argued analytically. 2) No empirical head-to-head baseline: Table 1 is a qualitative feature checklist versus prior schemes (Kirchenbauer; Christ/Aaronson/Kuditipudi; Wang/Abdelnabi/Yoo/Fernandez), and the reported BER numbers are for DISC alone. 3) Capacity is small in the study (m=1..4 bits). 4) Binarization imposes a log|V| multiplier on the number of tokens needed to hit target error rates (Eq. 14), an overhead the authors themselves flag; they note ongoing work to operate directly on the original token set. 5) Robustness is limited to a design-level argument against single-token deletion; paraphrase, substitution, insertion, or adaptive removal attacks are not tested. 6) Detection requires the secret key (not a public watermark).

datasets_used: Synthetic/simulated binary token sequences (per-bit probabilities randomly generated); no real LLM or text dataset used
Vocabulary size |V| = 50272 used for the analytical Lmin figure (Figure 3); one real token encoded as 17 bits in the experiments
quantitative_results: 1-bit watermark: 0 BER achieved over a text of 6 real tokens
4-bit watermark: 0 BER achieved at 20 real tokens
BER computed over 10,000 DISC runs per text length; message sizes m = 1 to 4 evaluated
Decoding complexity reduced to O(L^2), independent of the number 2^m of possible messages
PRF context n-gram sized so that floor(h/log|V|) = 5 (bounded by <= 8)
Distortion-free guarantee: P{y_i in accepting interval} = p_i(1) exactly, for any embedded message M (Propositions 2.5, 4.2)
Binarization overhead: required token count scales by factor log|V| relative to the non-binarized model (Eqs. 12-14, 38)
baselines_compared: Christ et al. (2023) zero-bit distortion-free scheme -- the direct predecessor DISC extends; paper also claims to correct a flaw in its statistical test/decoder and changes the PRF input to a context n-gram

Qualitative feature comparison (Table 1) only, no empirical numbers, against: Group A = Kirchenbauer et al. 2023a/2023b, Liu et al. 2023 (distorting watermarks); Group B = Christ et al. 2023, Aaronson 2023, Kuditipudi et al. 2023 (zero-bit distortion-free); Group C = Wang et al. 2023, Abdelnabi & Fritz 2021, Yoo et al. 2023, Fernandez et al. 2023 (multi-bit but not distortion-free). DISC is the only column claiming all of: high detection, low false alarm, distortion-free, multi-bit, low BER, efficient decoding.
